data.title: BP Q1 Results Show Progress, Not A Buy Signal
data.description:
BP p.l.c.’s (BP) Q1 2026 numbers make it hard to lean too bullish or bearish. There’s clear progress in delivering $3.2 billion of underlying RC profit, $8.9 billion of adjusted operating cash flow and high operating reliability, even though reported operating cash flow was held back by working capital. However, a lot still hangs in the balance: how they manage the big shift in strategy, whether they can actually close planned asset sales, and their constant exposure to swings in oil and gas prices.

My earlier view on BP was Hold, with caution tied to buybacks being suspended and extra cash being directed toward debt reduction. Since then, BP’s shares have actually risen more than 20%, while the stock is up 33.6% YTD; one news headline also described the stock as outperforming rivals since the Iran-war period. That’s come alongside profits bouncing back quarter-over-quarter, strong results in its downstream trading and a solid approach to managing capital. The real question is whether these gains are the start of something lasting or just a brief improvement while BP is still figuring out how to balance its old business with new investments.

Profit Rebound

BP’s Q1 underlying replacement cost profit hit $3.2 billion, jumping up from last quarter. The main reason was exceptional oil trading, stronger midstream performance, and a $1.7 billion quarter-over-quarter increase in products, while oil production and operations was broadly flat quarter-over-quarter and gas and low-carbon energy was slightly lower. Profit came in at $3.8 billion, much better than the loss posted in Q4 and about 5.6x what it was a year ago. Total revenue reached $52.26 billion, coming in $6.3 billion above what analysts expected. Earnings per share (ADS) landed at $1.24, beating estimates by more than 30 cents. These numbers point to BP getting a lot right in a tough, unpredictable energy market, especially by keeping upstream plant reliability at 95.7% and refining availability at 96.3%, with refining availability above BP’s 96% target for the fifth consecutive quarter.

Adjusted operating cash flow before working capital came in strong at $8.9 billion. But if you look at actual operating cash flow, it dropped to $2.9 billion. The main reason: a $6.0 billion build-up in working capital, mostly from stacking up downstream inventories for the season and effects from price swings. Management thinks around half of the $4.1 billion seasonal, inventory and rising-price component should unwind over the rest of the year, with most of that over the next two quarters, though that depends on how things shake out, both with geopolitics and how fast inventory levels get back to normal.

Production Holds

Production landed at 2,339 mboed, holding steady even with all the Middle East supply chain disruptions and after a North Sea divestment. Upstream got a lift from higher prices, BP averaged $59.75 a barrel on oil production and operations liquids and $67.17 a barrel on gas and low-carbon energy liquids. Brent sat at $81.1, but those realized prices still trailed the main benchmarks. That's mostly down to price lags, price caps, timing of liftings and contract structures; management pegs it at “around $700 million from price lags” on oil and $200 million for gas, if you match it up with their usual rules of thumb. Bottom line, if those lags keep up or get worse, future revenue probably won’t match the more optimistic spot price hopes.

Cost Cuts

Costs stayed under control. Underlying operating expenses were $5.369 billion, down from $5.639 billion last quarter, or roughly 5% lower quarter-over-quarter. That’s mostly from trimming overhead, pushing through ongoing cost cuts and sticking to the plan to slim down the portfolio. The Gelsenkirchen refinery sale, which BP announced back in March, will bump up their cost-cutting target to somewhere between $6.5 and $7.5 billion by 2027. That’s a real lever for them as they try to boost returns and make up for the drag from the energy transition. High reliability in upstream and refining, over 95%, helped too. That gave BP more operational leverage since they’re clearly putting cash generation first and holding off on less disciplined spending for now.

Balance Sheet

The BP balance sheet is still the main thing holding back the stock in the short run. Net debt climbed to $25.3 billion from $22.2 billion last quarter, primarily driven by lower operating cash flow after the working-capital build, with major divestment proceeds still expected later in 2026. Management still says it expects $9-$10 billion from selling assets in 2026, with about $6 billion from the Castrol deal supposed to close by the end of 2026 and 2026 divestment proceeds significantly weighted to the second half. That schedule matters: if any of it slips, BP will have a harder time getting net debt down into the $14-$18 billion range it’s aiming for by 2027.

Unless BP suddenly starts throwing off a lot more cash or those asset sales speed up, buybacks probably aren’t coming back anytime soon. That’s what both management and analysts are saying and you see it in the headlines, “BP’s debt boost could dash hopes of return to share buybacks” pretty much sums up the mood right now. Instead, BP’s putting capital toward the dividend.

BP’s 2026 plan keeps capital spending steady, landing between $13 and $13.5 billion. That’s down from last year’s $14.5 billion, with spending expected to be evenly weighted through the year. They’re using a revised $82.80/bbl Brent (CO1:COM) price assumption for 2026 impairment testing, while $81.1/bbl was the Q1 Brent average and expect production to stay about the same, mostly because issues in the Middle East are still weighing things down. Segment breakdown: gas and low-carbon output pull numbers down a bit, but oil production holds steady, refining benefits from a significantly lower level of turnaround activity, while refining margins remain sensitive to the cost of supply and conditions in the Middle East. BP says the underlying effective tax rate and results remain sensitive to the volatility of the price environment and its impact on the geographic mix of profits and losses. Management also counts on working capital coming back on schedule; if it doesn’t, cash flow and leverage numbers get tight.

Valuation

BP shares trade at about 9.12x forward earnings. The market seems to expect cash flow to hold up, assets to sell on time, and dividends to stay covered. Surprises could help, like selling Castrol or Gelsenkirchen sooner than planned, stronger results in trading or refining, or getting back to share buybacks if net debt drops as planned. On the other hand, delays in asset sales, worse Middle East problems (either from more lost volumes or lower prices), or transition write-downs that don’t get offset somewhere else could all hit cash flow and threaten the dividend.

Market Mood

Doubts remain, not helped by recent governance and shareholder-pressure headlines, including the chair’s re-election, rejected board proposals and climate-disclosure opposition. Management, though, is talking a bit differently now. CFO Kate Thomson said,

“As I reflect on another strong quarter, we are building momentum, delivering on our financial plan, all in support of our strategy. We are continuing to high-grade our portfolio and are taking action to reduce our cost base…We remain on track to deliver on our key targets by the end of 2027.”

That message leans on operational follow-through, but it’s still clear that the guidance depends on actually hitting those future milestones.

Risks

When commodity prices swing, realized prices, refining margins, the underlying tax rate, production and cash flow can all move because BP says its outlook remains sensitive to commodity volatility, price lags, contract structures and Middle East conditions. Timing on working capital and selling off assets can mess with cash from operations and make dividends less steady. Legal and regulatory headaches haven’t gone away, there are lawsuits in Kenya and over projects in the U.S. Gulf, for example.

Operations aren’t bulletproof, either. Supply chains are still shaky, especially with what’s happening in the Middle East. If it takes longer than planned to sell assets, net debt could remain harder to bring down toward the $14-$18 billion end-2027 target, which would weigh on the path back to larger cash returns.

Looking ahead to the second half of 2026 and into 2027, there are three main things to watch: whether the company actually closes on the asset sales it’s already announced (Castrol matters most here), whether free cash flow can consistently cover the dividend and then either a real pickup in share buybacks or a clear move to bring net debt down. If working capital loosens up like management says it should and asset sales go through like planned, BP might deserve a higher valuation. But if they start slipping on cash management, can’t pay down debt, or have to write down assets again, there’s still room for the stock to drop, especially if interest rates or commodity prices don’t help.

Button line, a Hold rating still fits here, not because I don’t trust BP’s ability to generate cash, but because the shift from fixing the balance sheet and selling assets to actually delivering higher, steady returns just isn’t finished yet. Any progress, whether from cost cuts, selling stuff off, or running the business better, has to show up clearly in earnings, cash flow and net debt trends. I’d upgrade BP to Buy if I saw real, steady growth in free cash flow and debt coming down, or if they got serious about share buybacks again.

data.title: Universal Electronics Has Multiple Paths To A Rebound
data.description:
A little over a year and a half ago, I made the bull case for Universal Electronics (UEIC), historically a provider of TV remote controls, but seeking to make a pivot towards the connected home electronics space.

With the stock trading at a discount to book, with a reasonable turnaround plan and a litigation catalyst to boot, shares appeared at the time to be a great opportunity.

Yet while UEIC appeared to be embarking on a comeback in the months following my write-up, since 2025 shares have coughed back these gains, and then some.

Instead of bouncing back from $8.50 per share to well into the double digits, the stock has since fallen by over 50%. Yet while this underfollowed and under-loved stock still gives off plenty of "value trap vibes," there may be merit in entering a position at current prices.

For one, there has been progress on both the patent litigation front, as well as with UEIC's transformation efforts. Valuation-wise, the stock remains considerably cheap, possibly making it an ideal buyout candidate for a strategic buyer, if and/or when the company makes further progress with its connected home transformation.

Finally, even if the transformation remains years away from fully taking shape, incremental progress, in the form of cost reductions leading to improved profitability, could also give this stock a meaningful life within a reasonable time frame.

With this, let's dive in and explore why it may prove worthwhile to take a second chance on Universal Electronics.

Recap and Recent Developments

In a nutshell, he's my initial bull case for UEIC stock: after years of steady revenue and earnings decline, due to the "cord cutting" trend, Universal Electronics knew full well that it needed to get out of the TV remote business.

Hence, the company began its pivot into HVAC and home automation technology products. While this did not result in a rebound in overall revenue, it did lead to an increase in gross margins, as well as a reduction in operating losses.

Again, alongside the turnaround catalyst, there was also a litigation-based catalyst as well. A favorable ruling in a lawsuit paved the way for Universal Electronics to pursue money damages against Roku (ROKU) and its TV manufacturing partners for past infringement on UEIC's patents.

Atop these two catalysts, there was also a factor that could be considered both a potential catalyst as well as a potential risk for the stock: the growing involvement of activist investor Eric Singer in UEIC, through Toro 18 Holdings, an entity owned by Singer's Immersion (IMMR). Immersion is a haptic technology patent licensing company that also makes investments in other public and privately held companies.

Given Immersion's track record of acquiring control of struggling businesses like Barnes & Noble Education (BNED) through restructuring, its growing involvement may not necessarily be to the benefit of existing UEIC shareholders.

Flash forward to now, and we'll take a look at each of these factors and how each played a role in UEIC's lackluster price performance since my 2024 writeup.

The Turnaround

Taking a look at Universal's 2024 full-year financials, it's easy to see why UEIC shares traded upward in the months following my initial article.

Although sales declined by around 6% that year, from $420.5 million to $394.9 million, operating losses shrank from $24.1 million to $7.2 million. EBITDA swung from negative $1.7 million in 2023 to deep in the black, $10.1 million, in 2024. Gross margins improved YoY, from 25.1% to nearly 29%.

Unfortunately, in 2025, progress was a bit more mixed. Yes, connected home sales reached $125.4 million, up 15.7%. For the year, the company also reported positive operating income, as well as positive non-GAAP earnings of $4.2 million, or 31 cents per share.

However, with sales for the legacy entertainment business falling more than expected, overall sales took a 6.7% dive for the year. As management puts it, the commenced home business still has yet to reach an inflection point.

Alongside a stalling out in terms of fiscal performance, another factor casting doubt on the UEIC turnaround has been about the company's current leadership, or lack thereof.

A year ago, longtime CEO Paul Arling retired. COO Richard Carnifax became interim CEO in June 2025, a role he continues to fill, as Universal has yet to appoint a permanent CEO.

Activist Involvement

Upon Arling's exit, Eric Singer became UEIC's board Chairman, further indicating Immersion's de facto control of the company. So far, Immersion has yet to make any public announcements indicating an interest in acquiring this company outright.

Interestingly enough, the two companies haven't even formed any partnerships, collaborations, or any sort of arrangement to monetize the patent portfolio. Singer, at last publicly, hasn't even made an effort to try and sell the company to a potential buyer.

Roku Litigation

UEIC's turnaround may be happening slower than expected, but at least the litigation catalyst has continued to play out favorably. As discussed in the company's latest 10-K filing, the court and the PTAB continued to rule in Universal Electronics' favor, with the PTAB invalidating the last of Roku's infringement claims. With these out of the way, and with the patent court already ruling in UEIC's favor years back, the potential for this company to receive damages remains on the table.

However, if a settlement is not reached, the case is scheduled to proceed to trial next March, as interim CEO Carnifax mentioned on the Q3 2025 earnings call. It's unclear how much Universal could receive in a settlement or judgment, but given its relatively small market cap, even an eight-figure amount could move the needle.

Valuation

Currently, Universal Electronics has a market cap of $53 million. The company has a total debt of $33.5 million, most of which is covered by the company’s $32.3 million cash position, giving UEIC an enterprise value of around $54.2 million.

Given the company’s TTM EBITDA of $9.7 million, the stock trades at a super-low EV/EBITDA of 5.6x. This represents a sharp valuation discount not just to other consumer electronics stocks, but also to profitable connected home electronics companies like Resideo Technologies (REZI), which trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of around 11x.

Not only that, I could see a strategic buyer being willing to buy the company at full value, or around twice the current share price, and still find a way to make it accretive, given the considerable overlapping costs that could be eliminated by consolidation.

Having said this, I don't believe that UEIC, under Immersion's control, will be looking at selling the company anytime soon. At least, not until the aforementioned Roku litigation is resolved. Even then, UEIC may be interested in pursuing further patent monetization activities. Singer/Immersion's cost basis is also well above current trading prices. I don't see Singer making an exit just to break even.

Moreover, I don't envision a lot of buyers wanting to gobble up Universal Electronics in its current "as is" condition. Much of the company's sales and earnings continue to come from the rapidly declining home entertainment business. It may not be until Universal is more of a connected home pure play that the company gets acquired by a larger U.S. or Asian-based competitor.

Potential Catalysts

Litigation Leads to Cash Windfall/Special Dividend

If UEIC's litigation against Roku results in a legal judgment or settlement, perhaps in the tens of millions or even hundreds of millions, the resultant cash windfall could change the story here for shares.

An amount at the lower end of the range could end up being used to pay a special dividend/buy back shares. A higher amount could be used either to pay down debt or to bulk up Universal's connected home electronics business.

Latest Product Launch Means Better-than-Expected Results

At the most recent Consumer Electronics Show (CES 2026) last January, UEIC unveiled its latest Next-Generation QuickSet homesense connected home application. Management is playing it conservative regarding this year's growth for the connected home business, but developments like this could mean better-than-expected growth for the segment in the coming quarters.

Better-Than-Expected Results, Even if "Inflection Point" Not Reached

Beyond the potential for new products to increase the chances of an inflection point happening, or at the very least drive better-than-expected growth, there's also the prospect of Universal delivering better-than-expected earnings, even as the legacy business keeps declining, and growth for the connected home business takes a breather.

Universal Electronics continues to right-size its two businesses and is engaging in further cost-reduction activities. Although management is not providing quarterly guidance for 2026, CFO Wade Jenke did mention on the latest earnings conference call that non-GAAP earnings are expected to come in between 45 cents and 65 cents per share, or 45% to around 110% above 2025's non-GAAP earnings of 31 cents per share.

If this pans out, this could dramatically improve the stock price in one of two ways. First, if earnings hit this range, the stock could receive a re-rating just based on its new forward P/E.

Instead of trading for 11.8 times forward earnings of 35 cents per share, the stock could trade at a mid-teens multiple on 45 cents to 65 cents, assuming modest valuation expansion. In other words, even without a takeover bid or positive patent suit-related news, there may be a path for UEIC to re-hit prices ranging from $5 to $10 per share.

Risks to Consider

Besides the risk that cost-cutting doesn't solve issues with home entertainment and new product launches fail to maintain the connected home's growth momentum, again there is the risk that Immersion, UEIC's influential shareholder, gains further ownership/control of the company without buying out outside investors at a premium.

Granted, it may be unfair to judge Immersion and its principals just on the BNED deal, but, as mentioned previously, it does serve as a sort of red flag for this stock.

The Bottom Line on UEIC Stock

While the situation with Universal Electronics has played out more as a value trap than as a turnaround play, if you've yet to enter a position, now may be an opportune time.

There's now less uncertainty surrounding the litigation catalyst. Even modest progress with the turnaround/transformation could also drive a big jump for shares.

Thinking longer term, the ties between Immersion and UEIC, previously viewed as possibly bad news for investors, could actually prove beneficial. With Singer at the helm of both companies, the investor, if opting not to fold UEIC into Immersion, could still utilize his talents/experience in the area of patent investing to better monetize UEIC's portfolio.

Put it all together, and the takeaway is clear. Existing investors may want to hold on, and newfound investors may want to jump in.

data.title: Travelers: Not Exciting, Just Reliable
data.description:
The Travelers Companies, Inc. (TRV) is rarely the most exciting insurance stock—but it is often one of the most reliable. TRV is one of the insurance stocks I have covered for years and one of my preferred dividend-paying insurance stocks, although I am not a shareholder—I can’t invest everywhere, unfortunately.

To wrap up briefly from my prior analyses:

  • With two decades of dividend growth, Travelers is not far away from dividend aristocrat status.

  • The company also repurchases its shares actively, with a reduction of more than 66% since 2006.

  • The company sold its Canadian operations in 2025 and redeployed the cash received to repurchase shares during the first quarter of 2026.

It is during that same first quarter that the company announced a 14% dividend increase, marking the 22nd year of consecutive dividend growth.

With all this information, I believe it's time to revisit Travelers' profile. Spoiler alert (I know I always spoil): Travelers, Inc. remains a strong candidate for investors looking for a financial stock.

Back to Normal. And It Shows.

Let's start with the underwriting performance—I am an actuary, so I am biased, even obsessed with the underwriting returns of property and casualty (P&C) insurance companies.

To be honest, Travelers' underwriting performance was exceptional during the first quarter.

The Q1 2026 combined ratio amounted to 88.6%, vs. 102.5% last year, which was affected by California wildfires, as was the case for most of the largest U.S. P&C insurers. Segment-wise, all portfolios performed well:

Q1 2026 Q1 2025
Specialty Lines 83.3% 82.5%
Personal Lines 82.9% 115.2%
Commercial Lines 93.8% 96.2%

The major improvement was in personal lines, with lower catastrophe losses than in Q1 2025 (9.1 pts vs. 40.9 pts impact), mostly from the homeowners' insurance portfolio. The personal lines' underlying combined ratio (i.e., excluding prior-year effects and catastrophe losses) improved by 1.6 points to 78.3%.

Commercial lines also benefited from lower catastrophe losses, while specialty lines remained steadily and boringly profitable, with a combined ratio below 85%. This segment remains the cash cow of the company, even though management has not neglected the other two segments and has succeeded in remediating both personal and commercial lines.

To be honest, the biggest news was the return to normal catastrophe loss levels, which positively impacted Q1 2026 performance.

The second driver of higher earnings was investment growth. Investment income reached $1.0 billion, up from $930 million in Q1 2025. For the quarter, investment earnings represented almost half of pre-tax income ($2.1 billion).

Capital Allocation: No Surprises, Just Execution

When you invest in Travelers, you know where you are going. You invest in a disciplined insurance company that redistributes excess capital over time. 2026 started differently from prior years. Previous years were good in terms of capital allocation; 2026 started excellently.

The quarterly dividend per share was raised by 13.5% from $1.05 to $1.10. But Travelers did not stop there. The company repurchased $1.8 billion of shares, including $700 million from the sale of the Canadian operations, as promised. Travelers does not oversell promises; it simply delivers what it announces. In total, the company returned $2 billion to shareholders during the first quarter.

Valuation: Not Cheap, Not Crazy

Travelers, Inc. is not cheap, and there are reasons for that. First, underwriting performance is more than decent. Even though Travelers' combined ratio does not match Chubb's (CB), Travelers' combined ratio is below 100% over the cycle, i.e., the insurance portfolio is profitable every year. Thanks to this profitability, fueled by highly profitable specialty lines and decent performance in commercial and personal lines, the company has grown its investment portfolio to $103 billion, generating $3.3 billion after tax in 2025, with a conservative allocation.

But what impresses shareholders most is the cash they receive, either indirectly through share repurchases or directly through dividend increases. And 2026 is a very strong year in terms of dividend raises. While I expected a 5% to 7% dividend increase, the company raised the dividend by 13.5%, more than double my expectations. It could be seen as aggressive; I don't think so.

If Travelers increased its dividend at a double-digit level, it is not because management suddenly became aggressive; it is because they believe the dividend remains sustainable, even after the sale of the Canadian operations, which were unprofitable—except for the surety business, which was retained.

Thanks to all of this, investors are willing to pay 13.56x earnings and 1.85x book value. Currently, Travelers is priced at around 9x earnings and 2x book value.

However, Q1 2026 was affected by the sale of the Canadian subsidiary; hence, first-quarter performance was somewhat boosted by this one-off effect.

Even when adjusting for the Canadian operation sale, Travelers does not look unrealistically valued.

To make the valuation discussion more explicit, I frame a simple fair value range based on normalized earnings. In 2024 and 2025, the company delivered post-tax income of $5 billion and $6.3 billion, respectively.

Based on a normalized earnings range of $5 billion to $6 billion, and applying a valuation range of 13x to 14x earnings, the implied market capitalization ranges from $65 billion to $84 billion.

At the current valuation (~$65 billion), the stock sits at the lower end of this range, suggesting limited downside and a potential upside of roughly 20% to 30%, depending on underwriting performance and capital returns.

That’s usually enough for this type of company.

Risks: Boring Does Not Mean Risk-Free

Travelers’ profile looks stable, but several risks remain.

First, catastrophe volatility. Q1 2026 benefited from normalized losses, but a heavy catastrophe year could quickly push the combined ratio above 100%, as seen in prior periods. The company is well equipped to handle catastrophe losses, but the market tends to have a short memory.

Second, personal lines execution. While recent improvements are encouraging, this segment has historically been volatile and remains sensitive to inflation, catastrophe events, and pricing discipline.

Third, investment income sensitivity. A large portion of earnings comes from the investment portfolio; in Q1 2026, it represented almost half of the pre-tax earnings. Lower rates or market volatility could pressure returns.

Finally, valuation risk. At around 9x earnings and about 1.8x book, Travelers is not cheap relative to its own history—on earnings multiples, it may look inexpensive, but on a book value basis, it does not. Any disappointment in underwriting could lead to multiple compression. Investors and the market could quickly react if the company fails to meet expectations.

What Travelers Is - And What It Is Not

Travelers is not Chubb in terms of underwriting performance. The latest Q1 2026 results confirm that Chubb outperformed Travelers. There is no doubt about it—Chubb delivers boringly steady underwriting results.

Travelers is not Progressive (PGR) in terms of premium growth.

Travelers is not Cincinnati (CINF) in terms of investment returns.

Travelers is not Aflac (AFL) in terms of dividend growth.

Travelers is an animal of its own, combining mid-single-digit premium growth, an over-cycle combined ratio around 95%–97%, and a conservative investment allocation. All these strengths have helped create value for shareholders.

For investors looking for balance, Travelers does the job.

data.title: Verizon Continues To Prove Its Quality
data.description:
Things have been going really well for shareholders of telecommunications behemoth Verizon Communications (VZ) since I reaffirmed the company as a Strong Buy candidate back in January of this year. From that time through today, the stock has skyrocketed 21.4%, easily outperforming the 3.6% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time. The latest move higher came on April 27th, when the stock popped 1.6% after management announced financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. Although revenue fell short of what analysts anticipated, adjusted earnings per share exceeded forecasts, and management increased guidance for the 2026 fiscal year. Relative to other similar firms, the stock is fairly attractively priced. And its low leverage makes it much lower risk by comparison. When you add all of this together, it makes sense to maintain the company as a Strong Buy candidate, even if the easy money has been made.

Calling all shareholders

Truth be told, the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a bit of a mixed bag for shareholders of Verizon Communications. Yes, revenue did increase year over year, climbing from $33.49 billion to $34.40 billion. However, the revenue generated by the company fell short of what analysts anticipated by the amount of $490 million. That is disappointing to say the least. But underlying this revenue growth ended up being some pretty attractive numbers. The number of wireless connections that the company boasted came in at 146.80 million. That's up from the 145.97 million that the business had just one year earlier. But that's not all. According to management, the number of wireless postpaid subscribers popped from 125.74 million to 126.50 million year over year. However, the number of wireless postpaid accounts decreased from 34.70 million to 34.37 million.

There were some other metrics that came in strong. As the number of fiber broadband connections jumped from 7.58 million to 10.76 million, total broadband connections expanded from 12.43 million to 16.76 million on a year-over-year basis. Also helpful ended up being FWA broadband connections, which jumped 24% from 4.85 million to 6.01 million. Management has been investing heavily in expanding its fiber network. The business ended 2025 with 30 million fiber passings. This year, the company expects to grow that to 32 million, before ultimately expanding it to between 40 million and 50 million over the next few years. This represents, in my view, a very attractive growth opportunity for the company. But we should not be focused only on revenue. Because where the business really delivered was on the bottom line.

During the quarter, Verizon Communications generated earnings per share of $1.20. That's up from the $1.16 per share reported the previous year. And it meant a rise in net profits from $4.98 billion to $5.15 billion. On an adjusted basis, earnings per share expanded from $1.15 to $1.28. That ended up exceeding analysts’ expectations to the tune of $0.07 and translated to a rise in adjusted net profits from $5.23 billion to $5.48 billion. This was not all, though. Operating cash flow popped from $7.78 billion to $7.98 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $10.40 billion to $11.07 billion. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business grew from $12.56 billion to $13.40 billion.

While revenue growth is certainly helpful on the bottom line, what is perhaps even more significant in the near term is that management continues to push for cost-cutting initiatives. They believe that their integration of Frontier is going as planned. For context, Frontier operates as a provider of broadband Internet and other communication services in the U.S. The purchase of the company in January of this year at a price of $22.3 billion expanded the company's fiber broadband footprint to 31 U.S. states and Washington, DC.

Management mentioned in their earnings call that they firmly believe that they will deliver more than $1 billion of run rate operating cost synergies by 2028. This success even caused management to increase guidance when it comes to 2026. Previously, they expected between 750,000 and 1 million retail postpaid phone net additions this year. They have decided to keep that range but mentioned that it would likely be at the upper end of it. They also raised earnings per share guidance from between $4.90 and $4.95 to between $4.95 and $4.99. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of about $20.92 billion compared to the $19.87 billion reported for 2025.

If everything goes according to plan, operating cash flow this year will be between $37.5 billion and $38 billion. That compares to the $37.14 billion reported for last year and would likely mean $42.40 billion on an adjusted basis that strips out changes in working capital. In all likelihood, we are looking at a rise in EBITDA from $50 billion last year to $50.82 billion this year. If we use these estimates and the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how it's priced compared to two other similar firms. Using the 2025 figures, it is in the middle of the two firms that I compared it to on a price-to-earnings basis. The same applies to the price-to-operating cash flow approach.

But on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, the company is even more attractive than these other enterprises. If we use the forward estimates for 2026, the picture is even more appealing. Only on a price-to-earnings basis is it not as cheap as or cheaper than the two companies that I compared it to.

In addition to being attractively priced, Verizon Communications has one other thing going forward. After we strip out certain asset-backed debt, we are looking at net debt of $137.67 billion. That is up from the $111.97 billion that the company had in the third quarter of last year when I wrote about it previously. However, that is because of the aforementioned acquisition of Frontier. Even with that additional net debt on its books, the net leverage ratio for the company is 2.75 using figures from last year and 2.71 on a forward basis. As the chart above illustrates, both of these readings are substantially lower than other comparable firms. This means that investors get an attractively priced enterprise at a risk level that is lower, all else being the same, than comparable businesses. This certainly makes the company attractive all on its own.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, it is my belief that Verizon Communications is an appealing prospect. Yes, I do think that the easy money has been made by now. But I think that enough additional upside exists, especially in light of recent strong fundamental performance on the profitability side of things, to justify maintaining it as a Strong Buy candidate.

data.title: UAE Quits OPEC, Crude Oil Explodes To $100 - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

It is a pivotal week for global markets, and after weeks of confusing fundamental catalysts, some regime-changing news is gripping energy commodities.

The UAE just announced it will quit the OPEC+ cartel amid a sharp rise in oil prices, now above $100 per barrel.

The OPEC+ organization aims to regulate crude production and prices for mutual interests, but with the Middle East conflict completely changing the rules of the game, the regime installed in 1960 is progressively tumbling.

The cartel, now left with 11 members (+ Russia), notably including Venezuela and Iran, has seen large challenges in recent years with production disagreements, internal foul play, and some members not respecting their quotas and limits.

The idea is that the most powerful producing countries had already begun to try to price out smaller producers through overproduction and price declines - a trend seen in 2025, which brought oil prices to 5-year lows.

And this already led to the exit of Indonesia, Qatar, and others in recent years.

Add to this the largely divided geopolitics of recent years, with decades of peace turning into global instability, and you get the recipe for some market-breaking news.

The Iran war is priced to end in recent months, if not weeks, and in the macroeconomic game, there is no place for those who don't prepare.

If and when Iran turns in for a deal, there will be blood in the market.

While the diplomatic attempts and news are still in a stalemate, with the US maintaining its blockade on the Strait of Hormuz to chokehold Iran's economy, the world is still operating and oil producers are progressively preparing for what comes next.

Trump just posted that Iran was pleading for the US to reopen the Strait promptly to move on to the next phase of the negotiations.

And it seems that a large pumping frenzy will reward those who are isolating themselves from quotas and limits - see what's already happening in the US and Canada, and they are largely winning from these Middle East dynamics.

With changing fundamentals, the market is in a repricing mode and is subject to high volatility.

Let's dive into a multi-time frame analysis of WTI (US) oil to determine levels of interest and put the odds in the trader's favor to capitalize on the issue.

US Oil Intraday Time Frame Analysis

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

WTI rallied frantically since its Friday 17th spike down to $82, now trading 23% higher as supply droughts persist and the geopolitical cloud fails to dissipate.

While conditions allowed for a grind higher, at current levels, there doesn't seem to be much interest for bulls to extend the action higher, particularly after the recent news brought an end to the move-up.

Check out the large bearish divergence which may confirm an end to this ongoing rally.

Explore the trading levels, then take a closer look at the 1H time frame for a few scenarios.

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • $104 next mini resistance (morning highs!)
  • $106-108 June 2022 resistance
  • 2022 and Monday highs $117-120 (larger channel top)
  • Ukraine war spike $120-124

Support Levels

  • $98-100 resistance (now pivot)
  • 4H 50-period MA $97.30
  • War support $93.00-95
  • $82 Friday 17 lows
  • 2025 highs key support $78-80

1H Chart and action levels

Crude has formed an upward channel in recent action, but the most important development to watch is the fact that bulls could not push above its central line, hence maintaining weak momentum.

This hints at higher odds of a downside break, with confirmation below the 50-Hour MA ($99.13).

  • Breaking the channel should maintain a range-bound price action between ~$93 and $103.
  • Any break and close above/below these areas suggest renewed volatility and changing dynamics, necessitating further analysis.

Safe trades, and keep your eyes on the news!

data.title: American Century Focused Global Growth Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Global stocks declined during the first quarter. Heightened geopolitical tensions from the war in the Middle East weighed on markets, driving a surge in energy prices and raising concerns about inflation and economic growth. Overall, non-U.S. developed markets stocks outperformed U.S. stocks during the quarter.

U.S. stocks declined amid slowing economic momentum. Equity markets were pressured by inflation concerns, lower expectations for interest rate cuts and softer economic indicators. While business activity remained in expansion territory, it slowed as the quarter progressed, and consumer confidence weakened as uncertainty increased. Value stocks posted gains across market capitalizations, while growth stocks declined.

Financials detracted from relative returns. Security selection and an overweight in the financials sector relative to the benchmark, with positions in Ares Management (ARES) and Barclays (BCS), weighed on returns. Shares of Ares declined amid private credit sector headwinds. Barclays, the U.K.-based financial services company, dropped amid a broad pullback in U.K. banking stocks as geopolitical and interest rate concerns weighed on investor sentiment.

Health care detracted from relative returns. Security selection and an overweight in the health care sector, with positions in Boston Scientific (BSX) and Eli Lilly & Co. (LLY), weighed on returns compared with the benchmark.

Industrials helped relative returns. Security selection in the industrials sector, among aerospace and defense names in particular, contributed to returns compared with the benchmark. Positions in defense and aerospace contractor Curtiss-Wright (CW) and Howmet Aerospace (HWM), a maker of parts for transportation and defense industries, outperformed on favorable financials and analyst reports. Having no investment in select benchmark holdings also added value.

Key Contributors

BAE Systems (BAESF)(BAESY). A position in Europe's largest defense contractor contributed as NATO governments ramped up military spending due to rising geopolitical conflicts in Europe and the Middle East.

Curtiss-Wright. Shares of the U.S.-based defense and aerospace contractor advanced on better-than-expected quarterly earnings and favorable analyst reports. The company's position in the nuclear power industry is also expected to be a driver of future growth.

Howmet Aerospace. A position in this maker of parts for transportation and defense industries supported performance, driven by strong quarterly earnings results, a boost to its full-year outlook and analysts' upgrades.

Key Detractors

Boston Scientific. A position in the medical device company detracted from performance compared with the benchmark. Shares declined after the company's latest quarterly report showed that sales in its key electrophysiology division missed Wall Street's consensus estimates.

NEC. Shares of the Japan-based technology firm detracted amid rising tensions between Japan and China. Recently, China added 40 Japan-based companies to its export control and monitoring lists, targeting defense firms. Further, the threat of artificial intelligence (AI) to various aspects of its service offerings has weighed on shares.

Expedia Group. Shares of the online travel agency detracted after the company provided a cautious full-year 2026 margin outlook. Fears over AI trip planners also pressured the stock price.

Notable Trades

ASML Holding (ASML). We added a position in this semiconductor equipment maker after it reported results that suggested demand for its high-end lithography equipment has started to stabilize and is poised to inflect higher. We believe continued demand for artificial intelligence functionality will drive more advanced applications in semiconductors.

AMETEK (AME). Organic growth is poised to inflect positively, driven by businesses that have been the biggest victims of destocking. We also expect order momentum to build in the coming quarters. We believe that AMETEK's growth will likely also be supported by acquisitions as the backdrop for mergers and acquisitions improves.

Tencent Holdings (TCEHY). We exited Tencent as the consumer environment in China continues to be highly challenged. We have redeployed proceeds to other higher-conviction ideas.

S&P Global (SPGI). We exited our investment in the stock to fund other higher-conviction ideas. The company's shares have recently underperformed as investors weigh the risk from AI to its core business.

Top Holdings

The portfolio continues to invest in companies we believe are strong and improving and have improvement that is sustainable. Our process is based on individual security selection. Some of the portfolio's key holdings are highlighted below.

NVIDIA (NVDA). We believe this semiconductor company will continue to see strong demand for its chips, fueled by increased adoption of artificial intelligence, along with other long-term growth trends such as cloud computing and online gaming.

Alphabet (GOOGL). The parent company of Google is a beneficiary of the reallocation of advertising budgets from traditional areas to the digital space. Its dominant search business and large user base support this trend. Growth will also be driven by other segments, including its video website YouTube and its cloud computing operations.

Microsoft (MSFT). Microsoft is mainly a provider of enterprise software and services. The evolution of its business model toward subscription-based software and services has improved the predictability of its operating results and accelerated its growth trajectory.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSM). TSMC continues to reiterate its long-term growth potential, underpinned by demand for high-performance computing requirements. We believe that AI will be an incremental and meaningful growth driver for the company over time.

Mastercard (MA). Mastercard operates a credit/debit card network system. The increasing penetration of credit and debit cards is a key driver to the improving profitability at Mastercard. It is a beneficiary of a long-running secular tailwind from e-commerce and electronic payments.

Broadcom (AVGO). Broadcom is a global leader in semiconductor and networking solutions. Earnings growth is supported by AI-related capital expenditure, resulting in rising demand for custom AI accelerators and Ethernet solutions. Earnings are also being supported by revenue growth and cost synergies from the VMware acquisition.

AbbVie (ABBV). We believe earnings will inflect positively, driven by an attractive product pipeline and the declining impact of patent expirations on a major product (Humira). We expect the biopharmaceutical company to offset the headwind from Humira with growth from newer assets such as Skyrizi, Rinvoq and Vraylar.

CenterPoint Energy (CNP). We expect earnings will benefit from a significant step-up in capital expenditure spending. The company has the highest rate base growth of regulated utilities, largely due to population, demographic and industrial (business) tailwinds within its Houston Electric subsidiary.

BAE Systems. We expect earnings to be supported by rising defense budgets in Europe, which remain underfunded, and higher Indo-Pacific defense spending. We expect earnings to also benefit from the recent acquisition and margin improvement from operating efficiencies.

Curtiss-Wright. Business is inflecting positively for Curtiss-Wright, driven by increased nuclear reactor retrofit work in North America, share gains from defense spending and sustained margin expansion. Margins are expanding owing to tailwinds from operational improvements and favorable product mix.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth by investing primarily in large companies in the U.S. and developed countries outside the U.S.

data.title: JBND: Active Management Cannot Fix Structural Risk
data.description:
Thesis

Our view on JPMorgan Active Bond ETF (JBND) is driven by its core exposures. The fund combines intermediate duration Treasuries, agency MBS with negative convexity, and investment-grade credit with historically tight spreads. In the current macro regime, all three segments face unfavorable conditions at the same time. As a result, we see limited upside potential and increasing downside asymmetry across the portfolio.

Overview of the Fund

With the inception date of 10/11/2023, JPMorgan Active Bond ETF (JBND) is about 2.5 years old and has managed to reach a total AUM of quite a respectable $7.4 billion.

Unlike many ETFs, JBND stands apart due to its active management. It has flexibility across sectors, durations, and instruments. In theory, this allows the manager to navigate the environment more effectively. In practice, we would note that it introduces manager risk.

The portfolio is broadly diversified, combining U.S. Treasuries, agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), investment-grade corporate bonds, and other securitized products such as asset-backed securities (ABS), with over 1.5k individual holdings and a duration of 6.13 years.

However, this same framework is where the challenge emerges. JBND is not exposed to a single risk, but to a combination of risks that are currently under pressure at the same time.

Carry, which for years acted as a stable source of return, no longer provides the same cushion. Credit spreads remain relatively tight compared to historical levels, while rising refinancing costs increase downside asymmetry. This leaves limited upside from spread compression, but meaningful downside if spreads widen.

At the same time, active duration management has its limits. While the fund can adjust its exposure to interest rate risk, it cannot fully escape the broader structural pressures driven by persistent deficits and ongoing refinancing needs. In this environment, duration is no longer just a tactical variable but a structural constraint, in our view.

An additional layer of complexity comes from the exposure to agency MBS, which introduces negative convexity and prepayment dynamics.

For investors to fully understand, MBS are pools of mortgages, and in practice, it works like this:

When interest rates fall, homeowners refinance more aggressively. The underlying mortgages are prepaid earlier than expected, which means the investor receives principal back sooner and is forced to reinvest at lower yields. The upside that a standard bond would experience in a falling rate environment is therefore capped.

When rates rise, the opposite happens. Duration increases precisely when it is least desirable. This is often referred to as negative convexity, and it is structurally different from Treasuries, which have no such embedded optionality.

Also, note that ETF holds more than 15% of its assets in investment grade corporates. As we stated, corporate spreads are tight from a historical point of view. Here we see a negative optionality with limited room for spreads to tighten further and a lot of room for spreads to widen in the case of stress in the market. We note again that the fund started operating in October 2023, so it effectively missed the downturn of the market that other ETFs have faced. That can be best viewed with a United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) that is essentially at the same level since the fund's inception date. Also, please note that since the inception date, credit spreads have tightened considerably.

The active nature of the fund introduces manager risk. Unlike passive products, where exposure is transparent and predictable, JBND depends on the manager’s ability to continuously position the portfolio correctly across multiple risk dimensions. In an environment where all of these dimensions are simultaneously challenged, flexibility becomes less of an advantage and more of a dependency on precise tactical execution.

Ultimately, JBND represents an attempt to actively navigate the same structural forces affecting the broader bond market. However, in a regime where both interest rate risk and credit risk are unfavorably aligned, the room for consistent return generation is limited, while the potential for misallocation increases.

Also, we have an observation investors should be aware of.

We downloaded all ETF holdings and observed that the largest position of the ETF, with 4.19% of total NAV, is in JPMorgan U.S. Government Money Market Fund Other (MGMXX).

MGMXX has assets of $331.13 billion, and JBND in it is slightly less than 1%, based on the stated market value in the table above. There are notable differences between fee structure of this two funds.

First, we state the fee structure for the JBND.

Prospectus of JBND clearly stated that acquired fund fees and expenses or AFFE by the adviser and/or by its affiliates are paid by the adviser, not investor.

However, it is noted that Rule 12b-1 fees are not paid by the adviser but by investor. We feel that investors should be aware of this situation.

So why did the adviser structure it this way? We believe that a look on the portfolio of the JPMorgan U.S. Government Money Market Fund (MGMXX) provides with the answer.

As can be seen, more than half of the NAV of the fund has maturity of just one day. We believe that advisor uses this kind of setup due to operational reasons and uses MGMXX as an aggregator for managing ultra short-term liquidity. The minimum investment of $50 million supports our suspicions.

Risk to Our Bearish View

A faster-than-expected easing cycle or a recessionary shock could push LT rates lower, generating capital gains on the duration component. In that scenario, JBND could outperform despite its structural constraints.

Another risk we can imagine is in further credit spread compression. If the macro environment remains stable enough and recession risk does not materialize, spreads could stay tight or even tighten further. This would support the investment-grade exposure and improve overall returns.

Next on the bearish risk list comes from active management. The manager could actually successfully rotate across sectors, reduce duration, or lower credit exposure at the right time. In that case, flexibility is an advantage and generates alpha rather than risk.

Also, if short-term rates remain elevated for longer, the fund can continue to generate stable income through reinvestment and short-duration instruments, including allocations to MGMXX.

Finally, there is a risk to our core thesis itself. If fiscal pressures do not translate into higher long-term yields, or if the market continues to absorb issuance without repricing rates, the structural bearish view on duration may prove weaker than expected.

To Sum It Up

JBND is exposed to a combination of risks that are currently negatively aligned in our view. A duration of 6 years creates sensitivity to higher yields, while credit spreads remain historically tight. We view these results as limited upside from further spread compression and meaningful downside if spreads widen.

Active management is another source of risk. In an environment where both rate risk and credit risk are under pressure, flexibility becomes dependence on precise timing.

Exposure to MBS and negative convexity caps upside when rates fall and extends duration when rates rise. This creates an asymmetric payoff that we structurally do not favor.

The credit component adds another layer of negative optionality in our view. We think there is little room for spreads to tighten further, but significant room for widening in a stress scenario.

The fund has not been tested through a full tightening cycle. It was launched after the major bond market drawdown, during a period of already elevated yields and compressed spreads.

The use of MGMXX suggests operational liquidity management, but also raises questions around cost structure and transparency.

Finally: duration risk, credit asymmetry, and negative convexity combined with manager dependency create an unfavorable risk/reward profile make us initiate with STRONG SELL.

Market Commentary

During the first quarter of 2026, geopolitical developments spurred significant market volatility around the world. The conflict in Iran triggered a broad selloff in emerging market equities and drove energy prices higher. Despite this volatility, emerging market equities continued to outperform developed market benchmarks. However, gains were uneven across emerging markets: Brazil and South Korea were the best-performing countries, while India and China lagged. Even with the outperformance, emerging market equities remain cheaper than U.S. and developed market equities.2

Wide valuation dispersions across sectors and regions continue to present attractive opportunities for price-disciplined investors. That said, there is significant concentration risk within the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (MSCI EM), where the Information Technology sector now accounts for 32% of the Index's total weight, compared to 21% three years ago.

Portfolio Strategy

We continue to believe there is long-term value in AI-related investments but remain selective given elevated valuations. In the quarter, we trimmed positions in memory-exposed companies in South Korea—such as Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix—following strong performance and historically high memory prices. 3 We also sold the Fund's position in Hyundai Motor (HYMLF) after significant stock price appreciation, driven in part by a rally in Boston Dynamics, Hyundai's majority-owned robotics subsidiary with AI-integrated capabilities.

We added to the Fund's position in MediaTek (MDTK.F), a semiconductor design company specializing in consumer electronics. MediaTek benefits from its strategic expansion into the AI supply chain, including its collaboration with Google on custom AI chips, but in our view still trades at a reasonable valuation.

During the quarter, we trimmed National Energy Services Reunited (NESR) after strong year-to-date performance. When we initiated the position in 2021, NESR was trading at a steep discount. Since then, the company has navigated an accounting issue, delisting, and successful relisting on Nasdaq. While our original investment thesis has played out, we think the company remains interesting as its strong fundamentals and growth prospects have improved, especially following a major contract win in Saudi Arabia last year. NESR is one of the top five positions in the Fund. The portfolio's overall Energy exposure remains slightly overweight compared to the MSCI EM.

Elsewhere in the portfolio, we initiated a position in BBB Foods (TBBB), a Mexico-based discount retailer focused on private-label products. The company is owner operated with efficient operations and a defensible competitive position. We believe BBB is well positioned for profitable long-term growth.

Outlook

Macroeconomic and geopolitical crosscurrents are likely to drive continued near-term volatility across emerging markets. In this environment, we believe our disciplined, value-oriented approach positions us well to identify opportunities that others may overlook. We appreciate your continued confidence in Dodge & Cox.

Key detractors from relative results included the Fund's:

data.title: SCHD: Beating The Market At 3x The Yield
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Equity Dividend ETF (SCHD) is solidly outperforming the U.S. market averages this year. In a period where the S&P 500 has gained only 4.8%, the popular dividend fund has risen 13.5%, gaining closer to 14.5% on a a total return basis (the S&P 500’s price and total returns are about the same because the index has very little yield).

The outperformance observed from SCHD year-to-date is pretty remarkable when you consider the historical period we find ourselves in.

Early 2026 is about three and a half years into the “AI era,” a period in which massive investments in generative AI are expected to lift corporate margins and turn AI companies into ten trillion dollar giants. The AI “hyper-scalers” are expected to do $700 billion in AI capital expenditure [CapEx] this year alone, and many analysts think that this spending will drive massive returns.

Given all of the above, SCHD’s year-to-date outperformance may seem surprising. The fund is overwhelmingly concentrated in consumer staples, energy and healthcare. Two of its top stocks are energy stocks. Information technology–the industry that is getting all the press this year–is only fourth among the sectors in SCHD’s portfolio. Why is SCHD doing so well, then, and can investors count on this continuing?

We can see one clue to SCHD’s admirable year-to-date performance in the holding sheet:

The fund’s third highest weighted sector is energy, and energy is widely outperforming the market this year. Thanks to the oil supply disruptions brought on by the War on Iran, energy stocks have been performing very well this year, up 27% year-to-date as a class (28% with dividends included).

So, SCHD has outperformed this year due to relative outperformance in oil and a few other “legacy” sectors, as well as relative underperformance in tech.

The question is, can the outperformance continue?

History would likely answer that question with a ‘no.’ The S&P 500 has beaten SCHD over the last three, five, and ten-year periods. Looking ahead, the tech sector (which SCHD largely excludes) has a massive catalyst in the form of generative AI. One might conclude the picture is grim for SCHD.

But one would be hasty to make that conclusion. The big AI companies are all trading at nosebleed valuations, with the NASDAQ-100 currently at 30 times earnings and 8.27 times book. In contrast to this, the SCHD looks positively cheap! Additionally, certain emerging themes are bullish for SCHD, chief among those being energy scarcity and competition for critical minerals. Many of the Schwab U.S. Equity Dividend ETF’s top holdings appear poised to profit from these themes.

When I last covered the Schwab U.S. Equity Dividend ETF, I rated the fund a buy, citing its relative cheapness compared to the broader markets. Since then, SCHD has delivered a 14% total return, far ahead of the S&P 500 in the same period. I expect SCHD’s comparatively strong performance to continue. So, I’m upgrading my rating to strong buy. I will elaborate on my rating choice in the ensuing paragraphs.

Portfolio

As with any fund, SCHD’s portfolio is of paramount importance.

SCHD is an index fund, tracking the Dow Jones US Dividend 100 Index list of companies. As you might expect of a dividend themed fund, it has a higher than average focus on dividends, and an above average dividend yield.

Its top 10 stocks can be seen in the image below:

Currently, the S&P 500 yields about 1.03%. The stocks in the SCHD portfolio have a 3.44% yield, according to SCHD’s fund literature. When we look at the stocks above in detail, we find significant corroboration for The Charles Schwab Corporation’s (SCHW) claimed yield. In the table below, you can see SCHD’s top 10 stocks along with their yields (courtesy of Seeking Alpha).

STOCK

YIELD

Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN)

2.05%

UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH)

2.5%

QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM)

2.5%

Chevron Corporation (CVX)

3.8%

The Coca-Cola Company (KO)

2.8%

ConocoPhillips (COP)

2.76%

PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP)

3.66%

The Procter & Gamble Company (PG)

2.9%

Verizon Communications (VZ)

Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK)

3.04%

AVERAGE

3.21%

As you can see, these stocks have a 3.21% yield on average, corroborating SCHD’s claim of having an above average yield.

The mere fact of a stock having a high trailing yield doesn’t mean much. If the S&P 500 is going to deliver 10 times the capital gain SCHD’s stocks deliver, the latter stocks having a three times higher yield doesn’t mean much. However, the SCHD stocks have much more going for them than just yield, as I’ll show in the next section.

Valuation

Valuation is a major concern in the markets right now. The S&P 500 currently trades at its second highest P/E ratio since the top in the 2021 growth stock bubble, and its fourth highest P/E ratio ever (see chart below). It also trades at the second highest Shiller P/E ratio of all time. Investors justify these high multiples with the promise of high returns from generative AI. However, these investments are unproven. The big AI companies are still losing money on model development, and nobody even knows whether NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) will keep up its current windfall profitability if the hyperscalers are forced to slow down their AI investments. In an environment like this one, having a value tilt in your portfolio can make a lot of sense.

It’s in this sense that SCHD really shines. SCHD currently has an 18x P/E ratio, a 3.66x P/B ratio, and a 10.38x price to cash flow ratio. The S&P 500, by contrast, trades at 28 times earnings, 5.3 times book, and 38 times cash flow. SCHD certainly looks cheaper. And, its edge in cheapness does not come at the cost of profitability: SCHD’s return on equity [ROE] is a respectable 27%.

So, we can see a clear valuation-based case for investing in SCHD today. The fund trades at lower P/E and price/book ratios than the S&P 500; it has a high return on equity; it has exposure to thriving sectors like energy; and finally, it has a respectable 27% ROE. In light of all these advantages, SCHD’s 3.3% dividend yield is just a bonus.

Operational Fund Characteristics

Some final criteria investors need to look at when analyzing any fund–in addition to its portfolio, management style and theme–are its operational characteristics. These are the characteristics that determine how a fund is built and traded, rather than its investment strategy. Despite their apparently pedestrian nature, these characteristics can have a surprisingly high impact on returns, especially when they are large.

Some of SCHD’s operational characteristics include:

  • A 0.06% total expense ratio. “Total expense ratio” is all of a fund’s expenses, including management fees. 0.06% is lower than average.

  • A 0.03% bid-ask spread. A low bid-ask spread means that market makers can’t capture much of your return when they trade a stock or fund for you. 0.03% is rather low, but not quite as low as the spread on large S&P 500 funds. If you sell a $100,000 block of SCHD stock, market makers will likely pocket about $30. Not too bad.

  • A 0.02% premium to NAV. Funds tend to track their NAV pretty closely. A premium indicates that the price should come down a little; however, 0.02% is such a small premium that it’s probably not something to worry about.

  • 10.4% annualized volatility. This is lower than that of a typical S&P 500 fund.

The above characteristics argue that SCHD is a well structured fund. With its low management fees and bid-ask spread, SCHD is unlikely to send too much of its holders’ money to fund managers or market makers.

The Bottom Line

The Schwab U.S. Equity Dividend ETF is a fund whose time has come. At a time when AI companies are burning cash and trading at 30+ times earnings, SCHD offers a sensibly valued portfolio with plenty of yield to boot. The fund is performing as well as anyone could ask this year, and I expect the outperformance to continue.

data.title: Why The Probability For A Global Recession Is Increasing
data.description:
The Market - Ignoring The Energy Dilemma

Just when it appeared like the market was ready to go for a more significant dip following a challenging first quarter, stocks staged one of their most considerable rallies in recent history.

S&P 500 "SPX" Futures (SP500)

I wrote an article about loading up in early April, and it proved to be a fruitful strategy. However, I must admit, that I did not foresee such an immense market rally, especially in such a short time frame. The S&P 500 has gained about 14% (roughly a typical year's worth of returns) in less than one month.

Nasdaq 100 Futures

The Nasdaq 100's ascent has been even more stellar, as the tech heavy index surged by a staggering 20% during the same timeframe (in less than one month). In retrospect, the rally makes sense, as the market sentiment had become overly depressed, especially in the AI infrastructure related stocks, despite the ongoing Iranian conflict.

Iran Deal Or No Deal

However, despite the enormous upside in stocks recently, the underlying issues with the Iran conflict have not been resolved. On the contrary, Iran still has the highly enriched nuclear material, which it does not appear ready to give up willingly. The U.S. now has three aircraft carriers in theater, in addition to the blockade, and oil prices continue hovering around $100.

There is considerable risk for kinetic hostilities to resume, in my view, and the disruptions in energy markets are increasing the probability of a global recession. While the U.S. is somewhat insulated, it is not immune, and a global energy shock will likely reverberate around the globe, hitting the U.S. hard as well. Due to this dynamic, I've become more cautious on markets in the near term, as well as longer term. In addition to the uncertainty surrounding energy markets, which could eventually lead to demand destruction, there are other serious issues to consider.

AI - Taking Over Jobs

The AI takeover has already started. Many big tech companies are announcing enormous job cuts in order to justify their gargantuan AI spending, and also presumably because of AI optimization and automation. Mass layoffs could lead to the erosion in consumer sentiment and could impact consumer demand very negatively. While we may all benefit from AI innovation and automation in the future, the transition process could be very messy, and it may impact many people and markets negatively as we advance.

Jobs Market Likely to Worsen

The U.S. jobs market has already illustrated signs of softening. While we have seen some uneven numbers in the recent data, the jobs image could become worse as more companies cut jobs to increase efficiency. This dynamic could be extremely detrimental to parts of corporate America, and markets in general, as consumer spending worsens. Let's not forget that the U.S. economy is largely consumer driven, as consumer spending accounts for roughly 68% of U.S. GDP.

Consumer Spending Likely To Worsen

Consumer sentiment recently hit its lowest level on record. This dynamic is very atypical, as we typically see ultra-low consumer sentiment figures when we approach a low point in a bear market. However, to see consumer sentiment this awful when the market is surging to new ATHs is a problematic scenario, in my view.

This dynamic implies that while Wall Street is making money hand over fist, Main Street is struggling, and those struggles could worsen. Therefore, we have a divergence, a tale of two economies if you will. However, this divergence can't last forever, and in time, the market could revert to the mean, meaning considerable downside for the general market.

Additionally, sky-high mortgage, near record credit card, and other high rates are another negative headwind for an already exhausted consumer economy. Add increasing gas, fuel, and all other costs that higher oil prices impact, and you may have just the recipe to tip the U.S. economy over into a recession.

Monetary Policy Uncertainty

As the new Fed Chair was nominated, I wrote that the appointment of Kevin Warsh was a policy error, and I still believe that it was. Warsh is moving through the confirmation process, but he has been vocal about reducing the Fed's $6.3 trillion balance sheet, and was not in favor of QE when previously serving on the FOMC.

In other words, there is little probability for QE, especially as it concerns longer term treasuries, which bring down mortgage rates. We may even see quantitative tightening, as the Fed looks to unload its balance sheet, as Warsh implied he would prefer. As far as decreasing interest rates. This dynamic will be highly challenging given the protracted Iran conflict, and the enormous impact on global oil and gas supply and demand. Inflation could continue rising, and it's nowhere near the Fed's 2% target rate. In fact, inflation has been moving in the opposite direction, which complicates things greatly for the Fed and the market, in general.

Rate Probabilities For 2026

There is less than a 25% chance that we will see a rate cut before the end of the year. This dynamic may not change even as Kevin Warsh takes over as the new Chief at the Fed. Despite the jobs market being lackluster, which could potentially worsen due to AI and other headwinds, there is little wiggle room for the Fed to intervene because of the elevated inflation. Which brings us to the real bright spot in the economy, which is of course AI.

What Happens When The AI Buildout Slows?

We've been in the AI expansion cycle for some time now. We've watched stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) go from about $10 (split adjusted) in late 2022 to over $200 recently. We've seen Palantir (PLTR) go from $6 to over $200. The gains in the Mag stocks and the AI infrastructure segment have been amazing.

However, my number one question is what will happen when the inevitable slowdown comes? Hyperscalers can't spend hundreds of billions of dollars annually on AI infrastructure indefinitely, and there will come a point when the "music stops." The question is not if the music will end, but when, and there could be many people left standing, holding the preverbal bag in the end.

Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Apple (AAPL), are all reporting earnings in the upcoming sessions, and it's going to be very interesting what kind of AI spending plans these companies have left. There is also the debate whether there is too much spending, and whether it will pay off or not eventually.

Circular Financing Deals

We see many circular financing deals occurring, and they will likely continue to occur. However, I think that this dynamic is quite troubling. Circular financing is very interesting. Here is how it works. For instance, a company like Nvidia invests ten or thirty billion in a company like OpenAI.

OpenAI then goes back to Nvidia, and purchases tens of billions worth of GPUs from Nvidia. Also, a company like Oracle (ORCL) can get tens of billions in loans to build out data centers for potential future revenues from OpenAI and others. Again, going to Nvidia to purchase GPUs for the data centers.

What about if OpenAI won't be able to pay its future obligations for whatever reason? How would Oracle pay back its debt then? Who will Nvidia sell its GPUs to when the system simply has too much supply, and increased competition? Margins could drop, growth and profitability could decline.

Not many people seem to be concerned with this potential dynamic during this AI frenzy. However, if such issues arise, they could have significant systemic implications for the AI industry, the banks and other institutions involved with the circular financing, and the financial system, in general. Therefore, concerns persist, and I am more cautious on the AI segment here, as I believe we may be in the later stages of the AI infrastructure buildout cycle now.

One More Chart For The Road Ahead

Just take a look at the semiconductor ETF (SOXX). It's up by nearly 170% over the last year, but this latest move has been "extraordinary." The SOXX surged by about 50% just in the last month alone. Semiconductor stocks are surging like the AI boom will last forever. However, I am concerned that the market may be getting ahead of itself. The AI bubble will not inflate indefinitely, and with price action like this, I am becoming more cautious. I've sold a lot of semiconductor holdings recently, including even releasing my Marvell (MRVL), AMD (AMD), and Nvidia (NVDA) stakes. Now, while I still may get back in on pullbacks, I'm certainly not chasing the semis here now.

Moreover, I think a considerable pullback in this space and the broader market could occur, as not all is well with the economy. Furthermore, in the intermediate term, there is increased risk of a recession, and a potential market crash to rival the best of them longer term (next 3-5 years). We've done phenomenally well in recent years, especially during the AI run up with stocks like Palantir, Nvidia, Meta, Alphabet, Amazon, and many others. However, I don't think that now is the time to remain uber bullish on these and other high profile AI names.

Instead, I prefer to protect my gains, and take a more practical and a more defensive approach now, even if there is marginal upside ahead. The risks are starting to outweigh the potential rewards, in my view. Having said that, we may still have a solid year in 2026, but due to the increased uncertainty I am widening my 2026 year-end target range to 7,200-7,700.
